source_paper_title: A New Dynamic Programming Approach for Spanning Trees with Chain Constraints and Beyond
source_paper_bibtex: @article{N_gele_2024, title={A New Dynamic Programming Approach for Spanning Trees with Chain Constraints and Beyond}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0012}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0012}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nägele, Martin and Zenklusen, Rico}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2078–2108} }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (discussion of prior work on laminar cut constraints following results of Bansal et al. and Olver–Zenklusen).

As later shown by Olver and Zenklusen [OZ18], this is almost optimal because an additive violation of \(c \log |V|/\log\log |V|\) units, for some constant \(c\), cannot be achieved unless \(\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{NP}\). It remains open whether \(O(1)\)-multiplicative violations are possible.
title: Constant-factor multiplicative violation for cut-constrained spanning trees in laminar families
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an undirected graph with nonnegative edge costs \(c:E\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\). For a nonempty proper vertex subset \(S\subset V\), let \(\delta(S)\subseteq E\) denote the cut-set, i.e., edges with exactly one endpoint in \(S\). A family \(\mathcal{L}\subseteq 2^V\setminus\{\emptyset,V\}\) is laminar if for any \(A,B\in\mathcal{L}\), either \(A\subseteq B\), \(B\subseteq A\), or \(A\cap B=\emptyset\). Consider spanning-tree feasibility: a spanning tree is a set \(T\subseteq E\) with \(|T|=|V|-1\) that connects all vertices.

In a laminarly cut-constrained spanning tree problem, one is given upper bounds \(b_S\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) for each \(S\in\mathcal{L}\), and seeks a spanning tree of minimum cost subject to \(|T\cap \delta(S)|\le b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal{L}\). In the special case where \(\mathcal{L}\) is a chain \(\emptyset\subsetneq S_1\subsetneq S_2\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq S_k\subsetneq V\), this becomes the (upper-bounded) minimum chain-constrained spanning tree problem.

A common approximation notion allows multiplicative violation of the cut constraints: for \(\beta\ge 1\), the spanning tree \(T\) is said to have \(\beta\)-multiplicative violation if \(|T\cap \delta(S)|\le \beta\, b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal{L}\). (Cost may be compared to the optimum cost among spanning trees satisfying all constraints exactly.)
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a constant \(\beta=O(1)\) and a polynomial-time algorithm that, for every instance \((G=(V,E),c,\mathcal{L},(b_S)_{S\in\mathcal{L}})\) with laminar \(\mathcal{L}\), outputs a spanning tree \(T\subseteq E\) such that
\[|T\cap \delta(S)|\le \beta\, b_S \quad\text{for all } S\in\mathcal{L},\]
while having cost
\[c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT}),\]
where \(\mathrm{OPT}\) denotes a minimum-cost spanning tree satisfying \(|\mathrm{OPT}\cap \delta(S)|\le b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal{L}\).
latex:

For a nonempty proper vertex subset \(S\subset V\), let \(\delta(S)\subseteq E\) denote the cut-set, i.e., edges with exactly one endpoint in \(S\). A family \(\mathcal{L}\subseteq 2^V\setminus\{\emptyset,V\}\) is laminar if for any \(A,B\in\mathcal{L}\), either \(A\subseteq B\), \(B\subseteq A\), or \(A\cap B=\emptyset\). Consider spanning-tree feasibility: a spanning tree is a set \(T\subseteq E\) with \(|T|=|V|-1\) that connects all vertices.

In a laminarly cut-constrained spanning tree problem, one is given upper bounds \(b_S\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) for each \(S\in\mathcal{L}\), and seeks a spanning tree of minimum cost subject to \(|T\cap \delta(S)|\le b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal{L}\). location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (discussion of prior work on laminar cut constraints following results of Bansal et al. and Olver–Zenklusen).
open_problem_quote: As later shown by Olver and Zenklusen [OZ18], this is almost optimal because an additive violation of \(c \log |V|/\log\log |V|\) units, for some constant \(c\), cannot be achieved unless \(\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{NP}\). It remains open whether \(O(1)\)-multiplicative violations are possible.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for existence of a constant-factor algorithmic guarantee over all instances, so a single finite counterexample only refutes a specific proposed \(\beta\) or algorithm, not the existential claim. Hardness evidence would likely require complexity-theoretic reductions rather than automated instance search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate algorithms/rounding/DP schemes and evaluate empirically on generated laminar instances by computing exact optima for small \(|V|\) (via ILP/DP) and measuring worst-case violation and cost ratio. This can guide conjectures or discover promising constructions, but does not directly resolve the universal existence question.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Nägele--Zenklusen’s framework, the laminar cut-constrained spanning tree problem (upper bounds \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le b_S\) for \(S\in\mathcal L\)) is highlighted as a canonical setting where iterative relaxation yields near-optimal additive violations but leaves open whether one can obtain \(O(1)\)-multiplicative violations while preserving optimal cost \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\). Their paper achieves a quasi-polynomial-time \((1,1+\varepsilon)\) guarantee for chain constraints and extends to laminar families of bounded width, but the running time is quasi-polynomial in general and does not settle the fully polynomial-time, general-laminar question.

Subsequent work has made substantial progress on the multiplicative-violation side for general laminar families via “laminar thin tree” techniques. In particular, "Thin trees for laminar families" and Klein’s dissertation chapter on laminar crossing spanning trees provide constant-factor bicriteria approximations: they output a spanning tree with \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le \beta b_S\) for a constant \(\beta\), but at the price of a constant-factor increase in cost (e.g. \(c(T)\le O(1)\,c(\mathrm{OPT})\)). These results can be viewed as achieving constant thinness with respect to an explicitly given laminar family, aligning with the thin-tree motivations discussed by Nägele--Zenklusen.

However, the open problem’s stricter requirement \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\) (no cost blow-up) remains unmet by the cited forward work: existing constant-\(\beta\) guarantees appear to require relaxing the objective, while exact-cost guarantees are currently known mainly for additive-violation regimes (iterative relaxation) or for restricted laminar structure (chains / bounded width with quasi-polynomial dependence). A promising direction suggested by the trajectory is to combine laminar-aligned rounding/iterative relaxation (to control \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\)) with objective-preserving “alteration” or exchange-based local improvement steps (as in Nägele--Zenklusen) to try to remove the constant cost factor, but no such full resolution is present in the provided citation set.

sources.title: Thin trees for laminar families
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives the first constant-factor approximation for laminar cut-constrained spanning trees relative to the natural LP. In particular (Theorem 2.4), given a feasible LP solution \(x\) to \(\min c^\top x\) s.t. \(x\in P_{st}(G)\) and \(x(\delta(S))\le b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\), it outputs in polynomial time a spanning tree \(T\) with \(c(T)\le (2+\sqrt7)c(x)<5c(x)\) and \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le 22\,x(\delta(S))\le 22\,b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\). More generally (Theorem 2.5), for any \(\eta>2\) it outputs \(T\) with \(c(T)\le \eta c(x)\) and \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le \frac{(2\eta+3)}{1-2/\eta}\,x(\delta(S))\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for laminar families achieving constant multiplicative violation \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le \beta b_S\) while keeping cost \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\) (no cost increase). This paper directly addresses the laminar cut-constrained spanning tree setting and achieves a constant \(\beta\) (e.g. \(\beta<22\)) multiplicative violation, but it does so with a constant-factor cost blow-up: \(c(T)\le 5\,c(\mathrm{OPT})\) (since \(c(x)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\)). Thus it makes strong partial progress on the feasibility/violation aspect for general laminar families, but does not meet the open problem’s requirement of preserving optimal cost (factor 1).

sources.title: Finding Structure in Entropy: Improved Approximation Algorithms for TSP and other Graph Problems
sources.theorem_or_result: In Chapter 19 (with N. Olver), the dissertation proves a constant-factor bicriteria approximation for the laminar crossing spanning tree / laminar thin tree problem: given a weighted graph and a laminar family \(\mathcal L\) with bounds \(b_S\) such that there exists a spanning tree \(T\) with \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\), one can find in polynomial time a spanning tree \(T'\) with \(c(T')\le O(1)\,c(T)\) and \(|T'\cap\delta(S)|\le O(1)\,b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\). The chapter also yields existence/constructive results for laminar-thin trees (thinness w.r.t. a laminar family).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm for laminar cut-constrained spanning trees that achieves constant multiplicative violation \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le \beta b_S\) for all \(S\in\mathcal L\) while having no cost blow-up, i.e. \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\) where \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is the minimum-cost feasible tree. Klein’s dissertation directly addresses the same constraint structure (laminar families of cut upper bounds) and achieves constant-factor multiplicative violation, but it also incurs a constant-factor increase in cost (\(c(T')\le O(1)\,c(\mathrm{OPT})\)), not the exact-cost guarantee \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\). Thus it provides strong partial progress (first constant-factor bicriteria approximation) but does not resolve the stricter ‘cost \(\le\) OPT’ requirement of the open problem.

sources.title: Reducing path TSP to TSP
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a black-box reduction from Path TSP to TSP: given an \(\alpha\)-approximation for TSP, for any \(\varepsilon>0\) it yields an \((\alpha+\varepsilon)\)-approximation for Path TSP (Theorem 1). Technically it introduces \(\Phi\)-TSP (interfaces \(\Phi=(I,T,C)\)) and a “Boosting Theorem” (Theorem 10) that, given an \(\alpha\)-approximation for TSP and a \(\beta\)-approximation for \(\Phi\)-TSP, produces a better \(\Phi\)-TSP approximation with factor \(\max\{(1+\varepsilon)\alpha,\,\beta-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{8}(\beta-1)\}\), using dynamic programming over a laminar family derived from a minimum \(T\)-join dual.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a constant-factor \(\beta\) multiplicative violation algorithm for minimum-cost spanning trees with laminar cut upper bounds \(|T\cap\delta(S)|\le b_S\), while preserving optimal cost \(c(T)\le c(\mathrm{OPT})\). This paper is about approximation-preserving reductions for (Path) TSP and a generalized \(\Phi\)-TSP, not about spanning trees with laminar cut upper bounds. It does, however, use a dynamic program that guesses edges across cuts in a laminar family (Section 5, Theorem 17) and explicitly cites N\u00e4gele–Zenklusen (2019) as inspiration for guessing a constant number of edges per cut. Thus it provides technique-level inspiration (DP over laminar families / interfaces), but it does not address feasibility/cost guarantees for laminar cut-constrained spanning trees, nor multiplicative violation bounds.
